title: Chicken gumbo
subtitle: Louisiana stew built on a dark roux with okra and andouille-style sausage
excerpt: The Louisiana one-pot that begins with a dark roux, equal parts fat and flour cooked slowly until the colour of dark chocolate. Then the holy trinity, chicken, smoked sausage, and okra are built on top of that roux, and the whole thing simmers into a deep, thick, smoky stew served over white rice.
sourceNotes: Gumbo is one of the defining dishes of Louisiana Cajun and Creole cooking, with documented roots in multiple culinary traditions: the French roux, West African okra-thickened stews, and Native American filé powder. The dish appears in Louisiana cooking throughout the nineteenth century and is documented in early American cookbooks as a distinctly Southern preparation. It is now considered one of the flagship dishes of American Southern cooking.

content.text: The dark roux is the soul of gumbo. It takes 20 to 30 minutes of continuous stirring over medium heat, and the colour must reach dark chocolate, not golden, not peanut butter, dark chocolate. At this stage the roux smells nutty and complex, and the flour proteins and starches have toasted deeply enough to add a flavour that no shortcut can replicate. Do not walk away. Do not stop stirring. If it burns, it is bitter and unusable; start again.

Once the roux reaches the right colour, the holy trinity (onion, celery, green pepper) goes in and sizzles against the hot roux, stand back slightly, as this is hot. The vegetables cook rapidly in the roux and cool it enough to continue. From there the gumbo is built like a stew: sausage, chicken, stock, okra, and spices, simmered for 45 minutes. Serve over white rice.

items.name: Chicken thigh
items.prepNote: boneless, skinless, cut into large pieces

content.text: Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in the large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir immediately with a wooden spoon or flat-bottomed spatula. Stir continuously for 20 to 30 minutes, adjusting the heat as needed, until the roux turns the colour of dark chocolate and smells deeply nutty. Do not stop stirring.

Add the {{onion}}, {{celery}}, and {{pepper-green}} to the hot roux. It will sizzle loudly. Stir for 5 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and the seasoning ({{paprika-smoked}} of paprika, {{oregano-dried}} of oregano, {{thyme}} of thyme, {{cayenne}} of cayenne) and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{sausages-pork}} of sausage slices. Gradually pour in the {{stock-chicken}} of stock, whisking to prevent lumps. Add the {{chicken-thigh}} of chicken pieces and {{okra}} of sliced okra. Bring to a simmer and cook, uncovered, for 45 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the gumbo has thickened and the chicken is very tender.
Season with salt and additional cayenne to taste. Serve ladled into bowls over cooked {{long-grain-rice}} of rice, with the rice mounded in the centre.

items.fix: Start again, a burnt roux cannot be rescued. Keep the heat at medium, not medium-high, and stir constantly; it takes 20 to 30 minutes and cannot be hurried
items.cause: Heat too high, or stopped stirring
items.symptom: Roux burns and turns black and bitter

items.fix: Add the stock gradually, whisking vigorously after each addition until smooth before adding more
items.cause: Stock added too fast after the holy trinity
items.symptom: Gumbo has lumps of roux in it

items.fix: Simmer uncovered for a further 15 minutes; gumbo should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon heavily
items.cause: Too much stock, or not simmered at a steady enough simmer
items.symptom: Gumbo is too thin after 45 minutes

items.fix: The roux must reach dark chocolate (not peanut butter) to develop the nutty, deep flavour that carries the whole dish
items.cause: Roux not dark enough; a light roux adds starch but not flavour
items.symptom: Gumbo tastes flat despite the spices

content.text: Gumbo is Louisiana's most famous dish and one of the most culturally layered preparations in American cooking. Its name likely derives from the West African word for okra (ki ngombo), reflecting the West African culinary tradition of thickening soups with okra that enslaved people brought to Louisiana. The dark roux is French. The holy trinity is Cajun. The sausage is a descendant of the French andouille. Gumbo is, in microcosm, the story of Louisiana itself, a culture assembled from French, Spanish, African, and Native American sources that produced something entirely its own. It is served at festivals, weddings, and family celebrations across Louisiana, and it is widely considered the definitive dish of American Southern cooking.
